narration.default: In Scratch you had handy string blocks: "length of [Hello]" gave you 5, "join [Hello] [world]" gave you "Helloworld", "letter (3) of [Hello]" gave you "l". C's string library has equivalent tools — strlen (length), strcat (concatenate / join), strcmp (compare), strcpy (copy). One #include line at the top brings them all in. Today you'll use the four most common ones in a single program — counting letters, comparing two strings, joining strings together.

Line 2: #include <string.h> — same shape as math.h or stdio.h, just a different toolbox. Without this line, strlen, strcmp, strcat, and strcpy would all be unknown to the compiler — you'd get "function not declared" errors. The .h header tells the compiler what these functions look like; the actual code lives in the C standard library.

Line 7: strlen(a) — returns how many CHARACTERS are in the string, NOT including the hidden '\0' terminator. For "Hello" that's 5 (H, e, l, l, o). The return type is a special "size_t" (basically unsigned long), so we use %lu in printf. (For most beginner code, %d works too but technically %lu is correct.) Try strlen on a longer string to see — strlen("CodeSkool") = 9, strlen("") = 0 (empty string).

steps.title: strcpy copies one string into another

narration.default: strcpy(dest, src) — COPIES the contents of src into dest. Note: dest is FIRST in the arguments, src second. (It's like writing dest = src; in pseudo-code.) Why do you need strcpy? Because you CAN'T write a = "Hello"; once a is declared — assignment doesn't work for arrays. To put a new string into an existing char array, use strcpy(a, "Hello");. Important: dest must be BIG ENOUGH to hold src plus the '\0' — if not, you write past the end and corrupt memory.

steps.title: strcmp checks if two strings are the same

narration.default: Lines 8 to 12: if (strcmp(a, b) == 0) — strcmp returns 0 if the two strings are EXACTLY equal, non-zero otherwise. Surprising twist: == 0 means SAME, == non-zero means DIFFERENT. This catches everyone — you'd expect "true means same" but it's the opposite. (The non-zero return is actually the alphabetic difference, useful for sorting.) NEVER compare strings with == directly — that compares ADDRESSES, not contents, and almost always gives wrong answers.

Lines 13 and 14: strcat(a, " "); then strcat(a, b);. strcat(dest, src) APPENDS src to the end of dest. So after strcat(a, " "), a holds "Hello "; after strcat(a, b), a holds "Hello World". (Same as Scratch's "join [Hello] [World]" block, just two steps.) Important: dest must have ROOM for the new content — that's why we declared a[20] originally, plenty of space for "Hello World" plus the terminator.

strcpy and strcat do NOT check sizes — they trust you to make sure dest is big enough. If dest is char x[5] and you copy "HelloWorld" into it, you write 11 characters into a 5-character box — you've overwritten 6 characters worth of memory you don't own. This is undefined behavior and a HUGE category of real-world security bugs (called "buffer overflows"). Always size your buffer larger than the largest string you'll put in it. The safer cousins strncpy and strncat take a max length and stop at the limit — use them for input you don't control.

Click Run. Console: len(a) = 5, Different, Joined: Hello World. strlen counted "Hello" as 5. strcmp returned non-zero because "Hello" and "World" are different strings, so the else branch fired. strcat joined them with a space in between. Scratch's "length of", "=" check, and "join" all in one C chapter.
